title: Tietze extension theorem
content:
In topology, the Tietze extension theorem (also known as the Tietze–Urysohn–Brouwer extension theorem) states that continuous functions on a closed subset of a normal topological space can be extended to the entire space, preserving boundedness if necessary. Formal statement. If formula_1 is a normal space and is a continuous map from a closed subset formula_3 of formula_1 into the real number s formula_5 carrying the standard topology, then there exists a of formula_6 to formula_7 which by definition is a continuous map with formula_9 for all formula_10 Moreover, formula_11 may be chosen such that that is, if formula_6 is bounded then formula_11 may be chosen to be bounded (with the same bound as formula_6). History.

L. E. J. Brouwer and Henri Lebesgue proved a special case of the theorem, when formula_1 is a finite-dimensional real vector space. Heinrich Tietze extended it to all metric space s, and Paul Urysohn proved the theorem as stated here, for normal topological spaces. Equivalent statements. This theorem is equivalent to Urysohn's lemma (which is also equivalent to the normality of the space) and is widely applicable, since all metric space s and all compact Hausdorff space s are normal. It can be generalized by replacing formula_5 with formula_18 for some indexing set formula_19 any retract of formula_20 or any normal absolute retract whatsoever. Variations.

If formula_1 is a metric space, formula_3 a non-empty subset of formula_1 and formula_2 is a Lipschitz continuous function with Lipschitz constant formula_25 then formula_6 can be extended to a Lipschitz continuous function formula_8 with same constant formula_28 This theorem is also valid for Hölder continuous functions, that is, if formula_2 is Hölder continuous function with constant less than or equal to formula_30 then formula_6 can be extended to a Hölder continuous function formula_8 with the same constant.

Another variant (in fact, generalization) of Tietze's theorem is due to Z. Ercan: Let formula_3 be a closed subset of a topological space formula_34 If formula_35 is an upper semicontinuous function, formula_36 is a lower semicontinuous function, and formula_37 a continuous function such that formula_38 for each formula_39 and formula_40 for each formula_41 then there is a continuous extension formula_42 of formula_43 such that formula_44 for each formula_45 This theorem is also valid with some additional hypothesis if formula_5 is replaced by a general locally solid Riesz space.
